analogy: Think of "Motion in a Plane" through the chapter's own opening images: CHAPTER FOUR LAWS OF MOTION 4.1 INTRODUCTION In the preceding Chapter, our concern was to describe the motion of a particle in space quantitatively. We saw that uniform motion needs the concept of velocity alone whereas non-uniform motion requires the concept of acceleration in addition.... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: CHAPTER FOUR, LAWS OF MOTION, 4.1 INTRODUCTION, 4.2 Aristotle’s fallacy. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Motion in a Plane is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that cHAPTER FOUR LAWS OF MOTION 4.1 INTRODUCTION In the preceding Chapter, our concern was to describe the motion of a particle in space quantitatively.

The text explains that we saw that uniform motion needs the concept of velocity alone whereas non-uniform motion requires the concept of acceleration in addition.

The text explains that so far, we have not asked the question as to what governs the motion of bodies.

The text explains that in this chapter, we turn to this basic question.

The text explains that let us first guess the answer based on our common experience.

The text explains that to move a football at rest, someone must kick it.

The text explains that to throw a stone upwards, one has to give it an upward push.

The text explains that a breeze causes the branches of a tree to swing; a strong wind can even move heavy objects.

The text explains that a boat moves in a flowing river without anyone rowing it.

The text explains that clearly, some external agency is needed to provide force to move a body from rest.

The text explains that likewise, an external force is needed also to retard or stop motion.

The text explains that you can stop a ball rolling down an inclined plane by applying a force against the direction of its motion.

The text explains that in these examples, the external agency of force (hands, wind, stream, etc) is in contact with the object.

The text explains that a stone released from the top of a building accelerates downward due to the gravitational pull of the earth.

The text explains that a bar magnet can attract an iron nail from a distance.

The text explains that this shows that external agencies (e.g. gravitational and magnetic forces ) can exert force on a body even from a distance.

The text explains that pHYSICS 50 4.2 ARISTOTLE’S FALLACY The question posed above appears to be simple.

The text explains that indeed, the correct answer to this question given by Galileo in the seventeenth century was the foundation of Newtonian mechanics, which signalled the birth of modern science.

The text explains that to counter this force, the child has to apply an external force on the car in the direction of motion.

The text explains that this explains why forces by external agencies are necessary to overcome the frictional forces to keep bodies in uniform motion.

The text explains that this task was Ideas on Motion in Ancient Indian Science Ancient Indian thinkers had arrived at an elaborate system of ideas on motion.

The text explains that force, the cause of motion, was thought to be of different kinds : force due to continuous pressure (nodan), as the force of wind on a sailing vessel; impact (abhighat), as when a potter’s rod strikes the wheel; persistent tendency (sanskara) to move in a straight line(vega) or restoration of shape in an elastic body; transmitted force by a string, rod, etc.

The text explains that the difference between a wave and a current (of water) was clearly understood; a current is a motion of particles of water under gravity and fluidity while a wave results from the transmission of vibrations of water particles. accomplished almost single-handedly by Isaac Newton, one of the greatest scientists of all times.

The text explains that for example, a spaceship out in interstellar space, far from all other objects and with all its rockets turned off, has no net external force acting on it.

The text explains that for terrestrial phenomena, in particular, every object experiences gravitational force due to the earth.

The text explains that if then, on earth, an object is at rest or in uniform linear motion, it is not because there are no forces acting on it, but because the various external forces cancel out i.e. add up to zero net external force.

The text explains that one reason is that the cricketer allows a longer time for his hands to stop the ball.

The text explains that in the examples so far, momentum and change in momentum both have the same direction.

The text explains that suppose a stone is rotated with uniform speed in a horizontal plane by means of a string, the magnitude of momentum is fixed, but its direction changes (Fig.

The text explains that 4.4 Force is necessary for changing the direction of momentum, even if its magnitude is constant.

The text explains that these qualitative observations lead to the second law of motion expressed by Newton as follows : The rate of change of momentum of a body is directly proportional to the applied force and takes place in the direction in which the force acts.

The text explains that it is equivalent to three equations, one for each component of the vectors : F p t ma x x x = = d d F p t ma y y y = = d d z z z a m t p F = = d d (4.6) This means that if a force is not parallel to the velocity of the body, but makes some angle with it, it changes only the component of velocity along the direction of force.

The text explains that it turns out, however, that the law in the same form applies to a rigid body or, even more generally, to a system of particles.

The text explains that in that case, F refers to the total external force on the system and a refers to the acceleration of the system as a whole.

The text explains that more precisely, a is the acceleration of the centre of mass of the system about which we shall study in detail in Chapter 6.

The text explains that any internal forces in the system are not to be included in F.

The text explains that acceleration here and now is determined by the force here and now, not by any history of the motion of the particle (See Fig.

The text explains that (4.5) F = ma = mg Thus the given equation describes the motion of a particle under acceleration due to gravity and y is the position coordinate in the direction of g. ⊳ Impulse We sometimes encounter examples where a large force acts for a very short duration producing a finite change in momentum of the body.

The text explains that in the history of science, impulsive forces were put in a conceptually different category from ordinary forces.

The text explains that example 4.4 A batsman hits back a ball straight in the direction of the bowler without changing its initial speed of 12 m s–1.

The text explains that (Assume linear motion of the ball) Answer Change in momentum = 0.15 × 12–(–0.15×12) = 3.6 N s, Impulse = 3.6 N s, in the direction from the batsman to the bowler.

The text explains that a natural question is: Does A in turn give rise to an external force on B ?

The text explains that the earth pulls a stone downwards due to gravity.

The text explains that the answer is not obvious since we hardly see the effect of the stone on the earth.

The text explains that the answer according to Newton is: Yes, the stone does exert an equal and opposite force on the Earth.

The text explains that we do not notice it since the earth is very massive and the effect of a small force on its motion is negligible.

The text explains that for the same reason perhaps, misconceptions about the third law abound.

The text explains that show that if it disintegrates into two smaller nuclei the products must move in opposite directions.

The text explains that 4.18 Two billiard balls each of mass 0.05 kg moving in opposite directions with speed 6 m s-1 collide and rebound with the same speed.

The text explains that what is the impulse imparted to each ball due to the other ?

The text explains that 4.19 A shell of mass 0.020 kg is fired by a gun of mass 100 kg.

The text explains that if the muzzle speed of the shell is 80 m s-1, what is the recoil speed of the gun ?

Source: NCERT Class 11 Physics, Chapter 4: Motion in a Plane

revision_bullets: CHAPTER FOUR LAWS OF MOTION 4.1 INTRODUCTION In the preceding Chapter, our concern was to describe the motion of a particle in space quantitatively. (NCERT Class 11 Physics Ch4 pg 1)
We saw that uniform motion needs the concept of velocity alone whereas non-uniform motion requires the concept of acceleration in addition. (NCERT Class 11 Physics Ch4 pg 1)
So far, we have not asked the question as to what governs the motion of bodies. (NCERT Class 11 Physics Ch4 pg 1)
Let us first guess the answer based on our common experience. (NCERT Class 11 Physics Ch4 pg 1)
To throw a stone upwards, one has to give it an upward push. (NCERT Class 11 Physics Ch4 pg 1)
A breeze causes the branches of a tree to swing; a strong wind can even move heavy objects. (NCERT Class 11 Physics Ch4 pg 1)
A boat moves in a flowing river without anyone rowing it. (NCERT Class 11 Physics Ch4 pg 1)
Clearly, some external agency is needed to provide force to move a body from rest. (NCERT Class 11 Physics Ch4 pg 1)
Likewise, an external force is needed also to retard or stop motion. (NCERT Class 11 Physics Ch4 pg 1)
You can stop a ball rolling down an inclined plane by applying a force against the direction of its motion. (NCERT Class 11 Physics Ch4 pg 1)

cases.name: The Greek
cases.point: The Greek thinker, Aristotle (384 B.C– 322 B.C.), held the view that if a body is moving, something external is required to keep it moving. (NCERT Class 11 Physics Ch4 pg 1)

cases.name: This
cases.point: This task was Ideas on Motion in Ancient Indian Science Ancient Indian thinkers had arrived at an elaborate system of ideas on motion. (NCERT Class 11 Physics Ch4 pg 2)

cases.name: Bhaskara
cases.point: Bhaskara (1150 A.D.) had introduced the concept of ‘instantaneous motion’ (tatkaliki gati), which anticipated the modern notion of instantaneous velocity using Differential Calculus. (NCERT Class 11 Physics Ch4 pg 2)

cases.name: The
cases.point: The difference between a wave and a current (of water) was clearly understood; a current is a motion of particles of water under gravity and fluidity... (NCERT Class 11 Physics Ch4 pg 2)

prelims_traps: Do not treat CHAPTER FOUR and LAWS OF MOTION as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Motion in a Plane; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Motion in a Plane through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use CHAPTER FOUR and LAWS OF MOTION to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
